topic: Data Preprocessing: Handling Outliers, Missing Values, and Skewed Data

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: A high-frequency trading platform processes transaction volumes that are highly right-skewed and contain frequent zero-value records (representing periods of zero trade volume for illiquid assets). An AI engineer is preparing this feature for a Ridge Regression model that assumes homoscedasticity and normally distributed residuals. Applying a traditional logarithmic transformation fails due to the undefined value of log(0).
questions.question: Which preprocessing transformation is mathematically most appropriate to handle this skewed feature without losing the zero-value data points or introducing arbitrary bias?
options: A) Box-Cox transformation applied after adding a manually tuned hyperparameter epsilon to all zero values.
B) Yeo-Johnson transformation applied directly to the raw feature data.
C) Standard Z-score normalization followed by an inverse hyperbolic sine (Asinh) transformation.
D) Quantile Transformation mapped to a uniform distribution followed by Min-Max scaling.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Yeo-Johnson transformation applied directly to the raw feature data.
questions.explanation: The Box-Cox transformation requires strictly positive data (>0). While adding a small constant (e.g., x + epsilon) is a common workaround, it introduces arbitrary bias and distorts the distribution, especially if there are many zeros. The Yeo-Johnson transformation is a modification of the Box-Cox power transformation that natively allows for zero and negative values. It stabilizes variance and minimizes skewness across positive, zero, and negative values mathematically without requiring arbitrary shift parameters, making it ideal for linear models sensitive to non-normality.

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: A smart-grid energy provider collects real-time streaming data from 10,000 IoT sensors. Due to intermittent network dropouts, approximately 8% of the sensor readings are missing at any given timestamp. The downstream anomaly detection model (a real-time Isolation Forest) cannot handle missing values (NaNs). The strict system Service Level Agreement (SLA) requires end-to-end inference latency to be under 50 milliseconds per batch.
questions.question: Which missing value imputation strategy balances mathematical rigor with strict operational real-time latency constraints?
options: A) K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) Imputation (k=5) computed on a sliding window of the last 100 historical timesteps.
B) Multivariate Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) using a fast linear regressor as the iterative estimator.
C) Last Observation Carried Forward (LOCF) combined with a pre-computed rolling median fallback for sensor-specific historical windows.
D) Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm to estimate the joint covariance matrix and impute values dynamically.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) Last Observation Carried Forward (LOCF) combined with a pre-computed rolling median fallback for sensor-specific historical windows.
questions.explanation: In real-time systems with 10,000 sensors and a 50ms SLA, iterative and distance-based imputation techniques like MICE and KNN are computationally prohibitive. KNN requires O(N^2) distance calculations, and MICE requires multiple iterative regression passes, failing the latency budget. LOCF is an O(1) complexity operation that leverages temporal correlation in streaming sensor data. Combining it with a pre-computed rolling median fallback handles edge cases (such as when the initial stream sequence starts with missing values) efficiently within microseconds, adhering to strict SLAs.

questions.scenario: Real-world Industrial Problem Statement: A cybersecurity firm is building an intrusion detection system (IDS) using a dataset containing 150 network traffic features. The dataset contains a mixture of multimodal distributions, heavy-tailed noise, and extreme outliers representing zero-day exploits. The engineering team must choose an outlier detection algorithm that avoids the 'curse of dimensionality' and does not assume a specific parametric distribution of normal traffic.
questions.question: Which outlier detection approach is best suited for this high-dimensional, non-parametric scenario?
options: A) Robust Mahalanobis Distance using a Minimum Covariance Determinant (MCD) estimator.
B) Isolation Forest (iForest) leveraging random axis-aligned splits.
C) Local Outlier Factor (LOF) computed using pairwise Euclidean distance metrics.
D) One-Class Support Vector Machine (OC-SVM) with a linear kernel and L1 regularization.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Isolation Forest (iForest) leveraging random axis-aligned splits.
questions.explanation: High-dimensional spaces (150 dimensions) render distance-based metrics like Local Outlier Factor (LOF) and Mahalanobis distance highly ineffective due to the 'curse of dimensionality', where distance vectors collapse and pairwise distances become uniform. Furthermore, the MCD estimator for Mahalanobis distance becomes unstable when the number of features is large relative to the sample size. Isolation Forest (iForest) isolates anomalies by randomly selecting a feature and a split value. Because anomalies require fewer recursive splits to isolate than normal points, it scales efficiently O(N log N) and works exceptionally well in high-dimensional, non-parametric spaces.
